7: David's words to Mephibosheth — ʾal tîrāʾ kî ʾāśôh ʾāśeh ʿimmᵉḵā ḥesed bᵉʿăḇûr yôhônātān ʾāḇîḵā wᵉhăšiḇōtî lᵉḵā ʾet kol śᵉdeh šāʾûl ʾāḇîḵā wᵉʾattāh tōʾkal leḥem ʿal šulḥānî tāmîḏ — 'Do not fear, for I will surely show you ḥesed for the sake of your father Jonathan, and I will restore to you all the land of Saul your grandfather, and you shall eat at my table always' — is the most concentrated narrative embodiment of ḥesed in the Samuel narratives. Ḥesed is the Hebrew term for the covenant loyalty that goes beyond legal obligation — the love that acts for the benefit of the covenant partner even when no reciprocal benefit is possible. Mephibosheth has nothing to offer: he is crippled in both feet (v3), the last survivor of the household that attempted to kill David, an object of potential political threat rather than benefit. David's ḥesed to Mephibosheth is motivated purely by his covenant with Jonathan (1 Samuel 20:14-17), not by any calculation of advantage. The phrase ʾal tîrāʾ ('do not fear') — spoken to the one who falls on his face before the king — is the standard formula of divine reassurance: the same formula YHWH speaks to the patriarchs. The table-fellowship that follows is the covenant sign: the lame man eating at the king's table as equals is the OT image that Jesus will deploy as the image of the kingdom banquet (Luke 14:21: 'Go out quickly to the streets and lanes of the city and bring in the poor and crippled and blind and lame').
1: Vayomer David ha-yesh od asher notar le-veit Sha’ul ve-e’eseh imo chesed ba-avur Yehonatan (“Is there anyone still left from the house of Saul to whom I can show steadfast loyalty for Jonathan’s sake?”). The word chesed (covenant loyalty, steadfast love, kindness) is the operative term of the entire chapter and appears in 2 Samuel 9:1, 3, and 7. Chesed is the relational word at the heart of covenant theology: YHWH’s covenant loyalty toward Israel and the human analog to that loyalty between covenant partners. The chesed David seeks to enact toward Saul’s house is grounded in his covenant with Jonathan (1 Samuel 18:3; 2 Samuel 20:12-17; 2 Samuel 23:18).
2: A servant of Saul’s household named Ziba (eved le-veit Sha’ul ushmo Tziva). They summoned (vayiqre’u) him to David; the king asked if he was Ziba. His response: avdekha (“your servant”) rather than ani (“I”) — the standard form of address to a superior. The name Ziba appears without etymology but will become theologically significant when his loyalty to David is tested and ultimately found ambiguous in the Absalom crisis (2 Samuel 16:1-4; 2 Samuel 19:24-30). His introduction here as a loyal servant sets up a later irony.
3: Ha-yesh od ish le-veit Sha’ul ve-e’eseh imo chesed elohim (“Is there no one left of the house of Saul to whom I can show the loyal love of God?”). The phrase chesed elohim (the loyal love of God, or: divine-quality chesed) intensifies the earlier chesed: what David seeks to do is modeled on, and participates in, YHWH’s own chesed. Ziba’s report: yesh le-Yehonatan ben nekheh raglayim (“Jonathan still has a son who is lame in both feet”). The disability is mentioned immediately — Mephibosheth’s lameness is part of his identity throughout (2 Samuel 9:13; 2 Samuel 4:4; 2 Samuel 19:26). He became lame when dropped by his nurse fleeing after the Jezreel battle (2 Samuel 4:4).
4: Mephibosheth (Mephiboshet, possibly a deliberate distortion of Meribba’al to avoid the Baal component; see 1 Chronicles 8:34; 2 Samuel 9:40) is at the house of Machir (Makhir) son of Ammiel in Lo-debar. Lo-debar (or Lidbir) may mean “no pasture” or “without word” (lo + davar/diber). The name suggests marginality — a place off the main routes, appropriate for a political refugee from the previous dynasty. Machir appears later as one of David’s supporters who provided supplies during the Absalom crisis (2 Samuel 17:27-29), establishing him as a wealthy loyalist in Transjordan.
5: David sent and brought Mephibosheth from the house of Machir in Lo-debar. The sending-and-bringing formula (vayishlach ha-melekh David vayiqachehu) is a royal summons. The journey from Lo-debar (east of Jordan, in Gilead) to Jerusalem involved crossing the Jordan and ascending to the city. For a lame man whose grandfather’s dynasty had been displaced by the man summoning him, the summons carried obvious peril. Mephibosheth would have had reason to fear a political execution cloaked in invitation.
6: Mephibosheth came and prostrated himself (vayipol al-panav vayishtachu). David called him: Mephiboshet — his full name. Mephibosheth replied: hinneh avdekha (“here is your servant”). The same hinneni/hinneh avdekha formula as Samuel’s response to the divine call (1 Samuel 3:4-6). Mephibosheth uses the self-designation avdekha (your servant) throughout his speech — the posture of total submission to royal authority. His prostration echoes the posture of the conquered foreign kings in the victory catalog, but this is a covenant act of grace rather than military submission.
8: Mephibosheth’s self-deprecation: mah avdekha ki phanaita el ha-kelev ha-met asher kamoni (“what is your servant, that you should give attention to a dead dog like me?”). The kelev met (dead dog) formula is the strongest self-humiliation expression in the Hebrew Bible. In the ancient Near East, dogs were scavengers, and to call oneself a dead dog is to claim the lowest possible social status. Mephibosheth uses the formula that Abigail used to David (1 Samuel 25:41: servant who washes feet) and that Ziba will echo (2 Samuel 9:9). The dead-dog formula appears when a lesser person is overwhelmed by unexpected royal favor.
9: The king summoned Ziba and transferred management of Saul’s estate to Mephibosheth: kol asher hayah le-Sha’ul ul-khol-beito natatti levn adonikhah (“I have given to your master’s grandson everything that belonged to Saul and his family”). The restoration of land confiscated from a defeated dynasty is unusual in the ANE; typically such land was redistributed to the conqueror’s supporters. David’s restoration of Saul’s estate to his heir enacts the chesed elohim he announced (2 Samuel 9:3) — a counter-ANE act of covenantal loyalty.
10: Ziba, his fifteen sons, and twenty servants are to farm the land and bring in the produce (ve-avadtem lo et-ha-adamah) for Mephibosheth’s household. Mephiboshet ben-adonikhah yokhal lechem al-shulchani tamid (“but Mephibosheth himself shall always eat at my table”). The repeated phrase “eat at my table” (2 Samuel 9:7, 10, 11, 13) becomes the chapter’s keynote: table-fellowship as the expression of restored covenant status. The king’s table is a place of honor and security (cf. Psalms 23:5: “you prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies”). Mephibosheth eating at David’s table as a son of the house is an act of political incorporation into the royal family.
11: Ziba’s compliance: ke-khol asher yetzavveh adoni ha-melekh et avdo ken ya’aseh avdekha (“your servant will do everything my lord the king commands”). The verse concludes: veMephiboshet yokhel al-shulchan David kachad mibenei ha-melekh (“so Mephibosheth ate at David’s table, like one of the king’s sons”). The simile ke-achad mibenei ha-melekh (like one of the king’s sons) is the highest status language: the lame son of the displaced dynasty is treated with royal-family honor. This is a type of what the NT calls adoption: those outside the royal household brought in as sons through an act of undeserved grace (chesed).
12: Mephibosheth had a young son named Micha (Mikha’). All who lived in Ziba’s household became servants to Mephibosheth. The mention of Micha extends the covenant: David’s chesed toward Jonathan (through Mephibosheth) is now extended one more generation — the grandson of Jonathan will grow up in Ziba’s household supported by David’s covenant provision. The narrative thus shows chesed operating across three generations: Jonathan’s covenant (generation 1) → Mephibosheth’s restoration (generation 2) → Micha’s inheritance (generation 3).
13: Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem, eating regularly at the king’s table (veMephiboshet yoshev biYrushalayim ki al-shulchan ha-melekh tamid hu yokhel). The closing notation — vehu pisequach shtei raglav (“he was lame in both feet”) — returns to the detail first mentioned in 2 Samuel 9:3, framing the chapter: the lame man who had every reason to fear the king now lives at the king’s table. The disability is not healed; it remains as a permanent visible marker of where Mephibosheth came from. His welcome is entirely a function of covenant loyalty, not his own capability or standing.
